name: 50 · Legacy File Crawl → Index → Register (file-ops crawl → couple catalogue + inventory)
description:
End-to-end demo of the file-ops `crawl` operation and the two-stage inventory lifecycle (docs/32 — the legacy file-migration capability). Nothing touches a real NAS: the first step SEEDS a small synthetic file tree on local disk, then the workflow crawls it, folds the streamed listing, INDEXES it (hashless observation), and finally PROBES + REGISTERS it (content-addressed) — the latter coupling each physical copy to a logical catalogue entry.

  Start{file_server_id, mekhan_url} → seed_nas (write ~7 files into /tmp/mekhan-crawl-demo) → crawl (file-ops `crawl`: recursive streaming walk, emits `{path,size,mtime}` batches over the `crawl` control channel) → fold (consumer-edge `join: gather` flattens the batches) → index (POST `/api/v1/inventory/index` — inventory rows ONLY, status `indexed`, no content hash) → register (hashlib SHA-256 each file, POST `/api/v1/inventory/register` — fills BOTH the catalogue AND inventory halves) → End.

The `crawl` op is `list`'s recursive, streaming, checkpointable sibling, built for the ~4M-file legacy corpus: it never buffers the whole listing, `stat()`s each entry for size+mtime (the OpenDAL `fs` lister omits them), and emits fixed-size `item` batches over the job EventStream — the same docs/25 consumer-join mechanism the streaming demos use. Here `batch_size: 3`, so the ~7 seeded files arrive as multiple batches; the `crawl → fold` edge carries `join: gather`, so the compiler's gather barrier re-orders and parks the whole collection as `{output: [<batch>, …]}`, which `fold` flattens to a flat `{path,size}` list.

The split into `index` then `register` is the load-bearing idea: registering an artifact is now a coupling that ALWAYS fills both halves of the equation — a content-addressed `catalogue_entries` row (logical identity, keyed on the SHA-256) AND a `file_inventory` row (the physical copy on a file server). You can never half-register: `/inventory/register` rejects an item without a `content_hash` (400). When all you have is an observed location and no hash yet (raw crawl output), that goes through `/inventory/index`, which writes inventory only and is honest that no content identity is claimed. So `index` produces 7 `indexed` rows with no catalogue rows; `register` then hashes the bytes (the runner is co-located with the files — the migration model where the edge probes and the server couples), promotes those same rows to `registered`, and inserts one catalogue row per distinct hash. All 7 files have distinct content, so `catalogue_inserted` is 7. The rows then show up in the app's Inventory + Catalogue views.

Run it against your dev stack: `mekhan run demos/50-legacy-crawl-register --server http://localhost:13100 --input start.mekhan_url=http://localhost:13100 --input start.file_server_id=demo-nas` (point `--server` and `start.mekhan_url` at your slot's mekhan port — e.g. 20100 for slot 1). It is self-contained (stdlib only, no pip installs) so it completes well within the `mekhan test` cap, but the index/register steps reach mekhan over HTTP, so the executor must be co-located with the seeded directory and able to reach the service URL — true for the default single-host `just dev` stack.
